caveats: a: BLS occupation data include captive operations outside the outsourced industry.
a: Process mix causes wide dispersion between automated lines and manual masking, polishing, or specialty-repair work.
rho: No public study directly measures five-year AI implementation in electroplating job shops.
rho: Regulated aerospace and medical programs should implement more slowly than low-criticality decorative work.
e: Industry classification does not prove revenue recurrence, profitability band, or transferability.
e: Some firms combine qualifying plating with nonqualifying fabrication, retail, or restoration activity.
s5: The owner-age proxy is not industry- or size-specific and measures owners rather than exits.
s5: Corporate, cooperative, and sponsor-owned firms have no individual-owner retirement trigger.
q: No public customer-contract sample was available.
q: Capture is stronger for qualified hard chrome, aerospace, and medical programs than for standard decorative or commodity finishes.
d5: The latest direct real-output observation is 2023.
d5: The aggregate mixes decorative, functional, repair, polishing, anodizing, and blasting services with different trajectories.
o: Necessary processing can consolidate into larger or offshore providers even if an accountable operator remains required.
o: Labor automation is captured in a and rho and not deducted again here.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring job-shop and production-program revenue billed per piece, square foot, pound, rack, barrel, batch, or quoted program, with charges for masking, pretreatment, plating metal, thickness, testing, certificates, packaging, and expedite service.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive finishing departments, firms fabricating and finishing only their own products, metal-coating services classified in 332812, jewelry-only shops, equipment and chemistry suppliers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market job shops repeatedly electroplating, plating, anodizing, coloring, buffing, polishing, cleaning, pickling, tumbling, or blasting customer-owned metal and other products for the trade.

a.bridge: BLS reports 31,700 plating-machine setters, operators, and tenders in 2024 and projects 27,400 in 2034, a 13.6% decline, while EPA and OSHA describe physical electrodeposition, bath operations, wastewater, and exposure-control work. I map quoting, specification and drawing review, order entry, rack and bath scheduling, chemistry trend analysis, certificate drafting, environmental records, quality anomaly triage, maintenance planning, and customer communication to AI exposure, while keeping masking, racking, tank operation, material movement, testing, polishing, blasting, cleaning, waste handling, and safety control largely physical. The occupational decline includes conventional automation, offshoring, and demand effects, so it likely overstates specifically AI-driven substitution.
caveats: BLS occupation data include captive operations outside the outsourced industry.
Process mix causes wide dispersion between automated lines and manual masking, polishing, or specialty-repair work.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task and time studies across rack, barrel, hard-chrome, anodizing, polishing, and blasting shops, mapped to installed ERP, line-control, laboratory, and wastewater systems, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The industry is labor-intensive and documentation-heavy, offering AI opportunities in order-to-process administration, planning, quality, chemistry monitoring, and compliance. Much of the work still involves physical part preparation, line tending, inspection, rework, and hazardous-material handling.

d5.bridge: BLS real sectoral output directly covers NAICS 332813 historically but does not forecast the lens's five-year demand. The index fell from 104.015 in 2019 to 89.848 in 2020, recovered to 96.938 in 2022, and declined 8.2% to 89.026 in 2023. Employment fell from 60,800 jobs in 2019 to 52,600 in 2023, remained 51,900 in 2024, and recovered to 52,600 in 2025. I use persistent real-output weakness and stable recent employment as a mild-contraction anchor. Output can fall while high-specification outsourced niches grow, so the proxy may understate stronger qualified segments.
caveats: The latest direct real-output observation is 2023.
The aggregate mixes decorative, functional, repair, polishing, anodizing, and blasting services with different trajectories.
d5.change_evidence: Current six-digit real output, customer-industry cohorts, production-program backlog, line utilization, shop closures, and five-year forecasts from aerospace, automotive, electronics, machinery, and repair customers would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Corrosion, wear, conductivity, appearance, and dimensional restoration preserve physical finishing demand, but offshoring, process substitution, environmental costs, and customer insourcing have pressured domestic real output. The base assumes moderate further contraction rather than disappearance.

caveats: Industry classification does not prove revenue recurrence, profitability band, or transferability.
Some firms combine qualifying plating with nonqualifying fabrication, retail, or restoration activity.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm census of the injected LMM population using external-customer revenue, repeat-program share, ownership, captive status, fabrication mix, and customer concentration would directly establish eligibility.
e.rationale: The industry definition explicitly covers finishing metals and other materials for the trade, which closely matches recurring outsourced service. Ineligibility remains for captive-like customer dependence, one-off decorative work, mixed fabrication businesses, and owner-centric shops that cannot transfer independently.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $3,280.2M / sectoral output (T30) $7,995.1M, year 2023

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $170,721 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332813); class <5: 533 firms, avg revenue/firm $393,627 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $77,230 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 364 firms, avg revenue/firm $942,525 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $184,923 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 349 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,895,158 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $371,830 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 513 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,356,768 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,247,198 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 103 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,637,816 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,245,339 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $35,119,353 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,890,417 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 650

caveats: Necessary processing can consolidate into larger or offshore providers even if an accountable operator remains required.
Labor automation is captured in a and rho and not deducted again here.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customer insourcing, material or coating substitution, additive manufacturing that eliminates finishing, or autonomous closed-loop plating cells that remove external accountable processors would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Remaining service quantity requires accountable physical preparation, bath chemistry, electrical deposition or anodizing, polishing or blasting, rinsing, testing, wastewater treatment, and release. Software can automate decisions and records but cannot place or restore the finish; some customers can insource or redesign parts and coatings.

caveats: No public customer-contract sample was available.
Capture is stronger for qualified hard chrome, aerospace, and medical programs than for standard decorative or commodity finishes.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract, quote, and invoice sample showing metal and chemistry surcharges, open-book terms, annual reviews, cost-down clauses, minimum lots, qualification costs, expedite fees, win rates, and churn would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Qualified processes, plating chemistry, thickness control, audit history, freight, turnaround, and failure risk create switching costs. Competitive per-piece bids, metal surcharges, annual customer reviews, alternate suppliers, and cost-down requests share part of implemented savings over five years.

caveats: No public study directly measures five-year AI implementation in electroplating job shops.
Regulated aerospace and medical programs should implement more slowly than low-criticality decorative work.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments at independent plating shops measuring quote hours, schedule performance, bath excursions, scrap and rework, inspection accuracy, record labor, accepted recommendations, and compliance findings would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Five years supports AI deployment in quotes, schedules, record review, chemistry trends, quality, maintenance, and customer workflows. Legacy systems, variable part presentation, qualification, environmental reporting, PFAS and chromium scrutiny, cybersecurity, and the cost of false releases keep human review and slow plant-control integration.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding US employer-business owners were age 55 or older in 2018. I convert broad owner-aging pressure into a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability and reduce it for family transfers, closures, internal reorganizations, minority recaps, owner-dependent customers, and environmental liabilities that prevent a clean sale. The all-industry statistic may understate aging in mature finishing job shops but does not measure transactions.
caveats: The owner-age proxy is not industry- or size-specific and measures owners rather than exits.
Corporate, cooperative, and sponsor-owned firms have no individual-owner retirement trigger.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and succession interviews across independent platers, plus five-year GF Data, Axial, PitchBook, and Capital IQ transactions coded for control, environmental diligence, and standalone continuity, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: A large independent job-shop population creates plausible succession supply, yet transferability depends on permits, wastewater systems, site history, customer approvals, skilled operators, and relationships surviving ownership change.

principal_driver: High labor intensity and repeat qualified job-shop workflows create a meaningful AI-addressable operating surface.
principal_weakness: Environmental liabilities and a long decline in domestic real output can overwhelm workflow gains.

sources.supports: Defines electroplating, plating, anodizing, coloring, buffing, polishing, cleaning, sandblasting, pickling, tumbling, and related work for the trade.

sources.supports: Reports 31,700 plating-machine operators in 2024 and projects 27,400 in 2034, a 14% rounded decline.

sources.supports: Defines electrodeposition purposes, identifies regulated metals and cyanide in wastewater, and describes current PFAS rulemaking for chrome finishing facilities.
sources.title: Electroplating Effluent Guidelines

sources.supports: Describes electroplating through electrical current and electrolyte solution, severe chromium health effects, and the 5 micrograms per cubic meter eight-hour exposure limit.
sources.title: Controlling Hexavalent Chromium Exposures during Electroplating

sources.supports: Describes ventilation and physical immersion-tank operations including electroplating, anodizing, pickling, cleaning, rinsing, and drying.

sources.supports: Documents industry processes, generated pollutants, treatment technologies, regulatory technical basis, and compliance costs for electroplating and metal finishing.
